sentences.text: They put us, hurriedly, into open flat cars, railroad cars.
We didn't know where we are going, or why.
May I say what happened to my friend in the hospital?
No, because that is, I think, important.
They were never taken away, because I told you there was an air raid, and the whole city was leveled.
And the place that they wanted these sick people be taken were also ruined, and the road so they couldn't move a car or anything.
And that's what saved my friend and many other people.
The air raid, that night's air raid.
And I think that saved me.
Maybe the SS man knew about what happened outside of the doors, you know, with the city.
So he didn't concentrate it on punishing me but I don't know, I just try to explain myself.
How come I wasn't punished more?
Anyway, in that place I want to mention other things, how foolish things can be done by people like me in a situation where I was.
In that very same place where we were-- oh, that is not important.
That was in Braunschweig.
Anyway, one of my friend of the 10 girls found a little mocha cup, you know, a little coffee cup.
And I loved that coffee cup.
So when we got the coffee in the morning, or black water.
They poured the water down to that cup, and I could drink it up.
The same block [INAUDIBLE], block [INAUDIBLE] lady who  took care of us saw the cup one morning when she was counting us.
You know, every morning a roll  call.
And she said, I will come back to you, and I want to have that cup.
The cup wasn't mine.
I drink my-- I drank my coffee.
I gave it back to my girlfriend.
When she came back, she asked for the coffee cup.
And I said, that's not mine, and I cannot give it to her.
She beat me merciless.
You know?
I thought, I never stand up.
But I never told her where is the coffee cup.
And I did things like that, what was really not a rational person, you know?
Because-- but I just didn't wanted to give it to her, because it was not mine.
I don't know why I mentioned that.
Something's connecting with that, I wanted to say.
Anyway, from there we did go to that factory.
And, when you asked me where we did go, and where did we go from the factory, you mean?
From the factory we did go to the open railroad cars, where the SS men in one corner of the cattle car was sitting, and with that heavy boots, were kicking our heads.
We are sitting around him, and we were outside some city.
They said we were in Buchenwald, not in the camp, but outside Buchenwald, in a forest.
And then the train was going all over the places.
We came to Berlin.
The city was completely ruined.
And then again, we were going.
We didn't stop anywhere to be unloaded and make a camp.
That was going on a few weeks.
Finally, we came close to Hamburg somewhere, and then, we were put into a private house.
But in Buchenwald we were asked to get off from the train.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the SS men made a ring around us, and with knife and scissors, were sticking into the people.
And two people got really mad, crazy, right then, when they're sticking-- I remember one of them had a daughter, about 14, 15 years old.
And she was terrible crying and begging the SS men, climbing on the SS men-- can you imagine how desperate she was?
To leave my mother alone.
But they were just laughing.
And then, for two weeks we were there.
We didn't get any food, or any water.
We ate the new, growing grass, and leaves.
And suddenly, they ordered us to, to make a line, and machine guns were lined up before us, and then they gave us one hot, boiled potato.
After two weeks.
Can you follow that thinking?
We ate the potato.
They told us that we will be dead.
And then, the second airplane came above us-- the first one in Braunschweig, the second in that place, what we called Buchenwald-- and dropped leaflet.
The SS men screamed at us not to pick up the leaflet.
But we did, and that was written on, don't you shoot the inmates, because we will shoot you.
Suddenly, all the SS men, except two, disappeared.
And we were left there before the machine gun, and ordered back to the train.
And we, again, was going somewhere.
And then we came to a place where a private house was empty.
And we were put there.
We didn't had any food or any water.
But after about, I don't know, six, seven days, 10 days, we were put in the train, that time in a regular person train, and was taken to Hamburg.
And then I was screaming and telling everybody, we will be free.
We are going like human beings in a train.
We will be free.
Where we arrived, also, to a big building, empty building.
There, after a few hours, or a day, later, people arrived with a Red Cross armband on the arm.
Later, I learned that was Swedish Red Cross, coming from Sweden.
Rescue unit took 2,000 inmates out from different camps, not only just us, because we were, in that time, very, very few.
We did go to Braunschweig, 850, and, no-- maybe we were, maybe we were, 40 or 50 alive.
And other camps came to that place, and 2,000 people were taken to Sweden.
That was 1st of May, and we were taken, with two SS men, to-- from Hamburg to Denmark, and we stopped by [GERMAN].
That's the name of the city, you said I should show you the soap.
I will go.
Yes.
And [GERMAN], when we had to-- I don't know.

Everybody left the train, or what happened, because my-- one of my girlfriends said she's dying, and I should bring her milk, because as we looked out on the window, the Danish people, God bless them, came with breads, and milks, and cheese, and I don't know what else.
Chocolate.
And she wanted to have milk, so I got off from the train.
And one lady gave me a bottle of milk.
But in the next moment, another inmate grabbed out of my hand.
And I started to cry, and the lady told me that, don't cry.
I will bring you another bottle of milk.
Just wait for me.
She had a lot of bread, also.

But I was looking around, and I saw the sky, beautiful blue, not one single cloud on it, and the grass between the railroad station and the village, what was a big, empty space, was green, and the sun was shining.
That was such a beautiful day.
So I forgot about everything.
I walked towards the village.
And then I came close to the village, the edge of the village, there were a young, in my eyes, that was an old man standing there, maybe 45, 50 years old, I don't know.
And he was looking at me and giving me money.
I was so happy.
He was crying.
I didn't know why.
And I was so happy for the money, because I, looked around, and there was street starting right, a half a block from where I was standing.
And there I saw a store.
So I did go there.
That was a bakery.
Three woman was inside, one behind the counter.
I put down my money, and I said that I would like to have milk.
You know, German milch and Danish maelk, but they understood what I said.
And she said, no milk, but packed a big brown bag full of bread, and gave it to me.
I had, then, blue-and-white striped uniform.
I don't know how and when I got that lucky, you know?
And a string was holding together my waistline.
So I took the bag, and I put in one side inside of my dress.
As I wanted to come out, I opened the door.
Two SS men walked by, still patrolling the street, because that was May 3, 1945, and the war still was on.
But, as I stepped out from the store, that-- the lady who gave me the milk was coming from the railroad station and saw me.
She was so happy.
She got off from the bicycle and said, come on with me.
And we did walk through the village to her house.
Up one floor-- one floor.
And she gave me a cup of coffee.
I never, ever forget that coffee.
I never, ever had, in the last 50 years, such a good coffee in my life than that was.
She had a husband and a little, blonde-haired girl or boy-- I can't remember what-- there, and she packed another brown bag full of bread, give it to me.
And she brought out clothes for me, and I said, no, thank you, because I am full of lice, and I don't want to dirty.
So I left the clothes.
I came down with her, and in the very same house where she lived was a store.
She gone in to buy milk.
She gave me  the bicycle to hold it.
There she is, inside.
And I was looking the bicycle.
I was wondering if I still can bike or not.
And when she came out, she told me that I should sit on the bicycle.
I said no, but finally, she convinced me.
And I did sit on the bike, and started to go.
And as I was riding on the bicycle, very  slow, so she could run after me, I looked around, and very low buildings, every window had a box full of flowers.
And behind every flower-box, that was a person waving handkerchief toward me, and laughing, and, and talking to me.
I don't know what.
I couldn't hear.
But they were so happy.
And then I came to middle of the village.
Then a railroad man came, and talked to the lady, told her that the train left for Sweden.
So she got very scared.
And I said, don't worry.
I am sure other, other train will come.
And I am going to the railroad station, and I will wait there.
So we parted, with the two bag of bread, and the bottle of milk, what she gave me, in my chest.
When I came to the middle of the empty space between the railroad and the village, there was a white van on the road, stopped.
And then a nun, or nurse, with the white outfit, came out from the van.
Later, I realized that was a Red Cross van.
And she was telling me that two other girls who run after me is inside in the car, and crying.
And she doesn't know what to do with them.
And I should come and sit in the car, because the train left.
So I did go into the van, and I told the two girls-- said, why you are crying?
We are free.
I felt right away free.
Matter of fact, I never felt like an inmate.
That was the strangest things for me to think about it.
And I was beaten many times.
And then we came to a hospital, where they cleaned us, and gave us beautiful, clean underwear, and a set of clothes, and put us in a hospital bedroom, with several beds [INAUDIBLE].
So we were-- each one of us had a bed.
The two other girls got on the floor.
She said, that's too comfortable.
I loved my bed.
And then, the nurses were wonderful.
The doctor came in, and gave money to us, so that next day, we should be able to go to a store and  buy something, if we wanted to, because he wanted to have that.
And the nurses gave us a soap.
Not too long after we arrived and was put into clean bed, and so about two hours later, three other person came into the room with the nurse.
Two woman with beautiful blonde hair, and well-dressed, and the suitcase, and about 17-, 18-years-old young, blond men, and the nurse said, they are inmates of concentration camp.
Naturally, we knew right away that no one dressed in a concentration camp like that.
No.
No.
We were bald.
I, I couldn't look in the mirror, actually, when I saw myself.
I had like a crew cut, you know, because the last two months, they didn't-- or last two months, two and a half months, they didn't shave us any  more.
And my hair was growing, and right away, a little curl in it, but very light curl.
So, anyway, I still had a crew cut.
So we knew that they are not one of us.
And the nurse left.
They started to ask questions.
I didn't speak to them.
The other two girls were telling, answering the question, which camp you are from, and what did you do, and so on.
Next morning when the doctor came in and said the new train arrived earlier than they thought, and now we have to go to the railroad station.
So we did go.

When we came to the railroad station, a new transport was there, I asked the leader, whoever organized that to go to Sweden, because the train was sent back to Germany with the SS men from [INAUDIBLE].
And we were on a new  train going to Sweden.
But in the first train where I did arrive, I didn't know, because the train left without me.
So I didn't know what happened, and how did the SS men get back, or anything.
But no, I did know.
So I talked to the leader that I would like to have a separate cabin, if I can, not with the rest of the inmates.
Because they were still itching and eaten by lice, and I didn't wanted to get lice again.
So they listened to me, and put us, the six people, into, to a small compartment that was a first-class train.
I don't know why.
And the compartment-- I don't know if you traveled in Europe.
I don't know, in America.
Matter of fact, in that time, in the first class, the package holder was like net.
And the German woman and guy laid down on the benches.
And the other two girls laid on the floor.
And I climbed up to the package holder.

And when we were traveling, the people, the German people thought we are asleep, I was looking down, and they opened up their suitcases, took out pictures from themself in SS uniform, and tear it apart, and threw it out in the window, and closed the suitcases.
They had cashmere sweaters and everything on them, wearing that, you know, and in the suitcases.
So I knew, definitely, 100% who they were.
When we arrived to Sweden, three men in berets-- you know, that hat?
Beret.
Beret.
Yeah.
Came to the train.
I don't know who these people were.
And they asked questions of that three German, who they are.
And they answered, German, in German, that they were speaking German.
They did say they are inmates of [INAUDIBLE] concentration camp.
They are Jews.
And, naturally, the three people didn't believe.
What happened to them, I don't know.
But one of them asked me that who are these people?
And I said, I don't know.
I didn't tell that I did see them on the picture in SS uniform.
I didn't tell they were German SS.
But I knew they were.
You know, even before I did see the picture.
Why?
Not because I was afraid, because I wasn't afraid.
Because I was so happy to be free.
I thought every one of us learned a lot from the war.
And I had several thought.
First, I didn't feel ever, and still, today, I don't feel any revenge in my heart against anyone, and against the German.
I feel hurt much more against the Hungarian people, because I felt I am one of them, and they let me be taken away, and be killed.
The German people were my enemy.
So, somehow, I didn't feel the same feeling toward them like toward the Hungarians.
Second, I felt I am not God, to punish anyone.
And I am sure, if that would happen today, I still would do the same thing.
Because when we were in Sweden, and we girls were put in Swedish empty schools.

You know that made it livable with beds, and kept us in quarantine, select out who are infectious, disease or whatever, there were a group of girls who were making a list about one girl, because she was in the camp, a [NON-ENGLISH].
And she was very, very rude to us, beaten us.
They made a whole list, and wrote a letter to the Jewish organization in Stockholm, to know about that person, and she should be punished.
Every one of the people in that convalescent home, we called it, signed it.
I didn't.
I am telling that story to you to understand that, against the Jews, or against the German, or against anyone, I never wanted to sign the death sentence, or punishment, or anything.
That the day I was liberated, and I came to Sweden, and I don't know what your next question.
OK.

I was in that convalescent home, and in hospitals, because I had many problems, but I did recover, and people came to the camp, Hungarian groups, you know, leaders in a Hungarian Jewish organization in Stockholm, and from the Swedish school system, or whatever.
Shall I stop?
No.
Came to ask questions.
What did we do before the war?
And I did tell them, and that I wanted to be a teacher, and I was tutoring, and so on.
They took my name, everything.
I didn't know why they asked the question.

But in 1945, December, I got a letter from Stockholm, that I should go to Stockholm for a week, because they want to test me, how much knowledge I have, because they want to make a school for children out of the holocaust, out of the concentration camp, and they do need teachers who speak Hungarian and German.
And so I was taken, with other ladies from different part of the convalescent homes in Sweden.
And after a week testing, I passed.
And I became a teacher in these schools.
And I was the first one to be hired, and the last one to let go.
Because during the time, as the school got smaller and smaller, the children wrote to the organization, if I let go, they want to leave, too.
So, that's what I did.
I was teaching two and a half years.
And children from age 10, that was just a few, till 20.
I had one pupil who was 20 years old, older than me, and she couldn't write, so I taught her how to write.
And she said that was her happiest moment in her life, when she could write a letter to her brother, whom she found out, during that time, you know, that they are alive.
So after the school wounded up, because the children were transported to Israel-- they did go, many of them, [INAUDIBLE], and to America, and wherever.
Some returned to Hungary.
I got, as a schol-- as a reward from the Jewish organization, a scholarship to do whatever I wanted to do.
I wanted to study to be a bacteriologist technologist.
And that's what I became in Sweden.
I did go, in Sweden, to college.
And, in the same time, I did work in a hospital right away.
What possessed you to do that?
To study bacteriology?
I don't know.
I wanted to be, originally, a teacher, because I wanted to teach the world to like each other, when the Nazis started and everything.
My mother was very interested in the medical field.
And maybe that triggered-- I don't know.
I just wanted to help people.
And I, I am still, today, very interested in everything new.
And especially now, for my husband, I, I wrote and called, plastic and other kind of factories and doctors, and UCLA, and everywhere.
Where I did go, I did work at UCLA.
I worked at USC.
I worked at McGill University at Montreal.
But what I was talking about, plastic factory and things like that, that is just now the last three years.

I am desperately trying to find somebody who could build something what I have in my mind, what would help my husband, who is paralyzed from the waist down, to make him able to stand up, so I would be able to put him in the wheelchair alone.
